_meta.confidence_note: Variety codes, classifications and disease-resistance packages are drawn directly from the Kutsaga 2023 variety brochure and the curated knowledge_data/tobacco_manual_kutsaga.json. PLANT-POPULATION ranges are grounded in the documented Zimbabwe spacing standard (1.2 m rows x 0.50-0.56 m in-row => ~14,800-16,700 plants/ha; Kutsaga spacing trials and Britannica Orinoco flue-cured spacing), with early/lemon fast-ripening lines toward the denser end and late large-leaf lines toward the sparser end (Phase 2 amendment for audit gap (a)). DAYS-TO-MATURITY (from transplant) and TOPPING-LEAF-COUNT remain partly interpolated from each variety's documented maturity class where the brochure gives no explicit figure — these two fields are still flagged in the per-variety notes and in research.md Section 9 (Research Gaps) and should be confirmed against the full Kutsaga trial tables. Yield 'optimal' figures are best-practice/irrigated potentials; 'typical' figures are realistic commercial-contract outcomes. Disease-resistance abbreviations follow Kutsaga convention: WM=White Mould (Pythium/Sclerotinia), WF-0/WF-1=Wildfire races 0/1, ALT=Alternaria, ANG-1=Angular Leaf Spot, RK=Root-Knot Nematode, TMV=Tobacco Mosaic Virus, GW=Granville/Bacterial Wilt, BS=Black Shank, PVY=Potato Virus Y.

varieties.notes: Similar to KE1 but with longer leaves; medium-to-fast ripening; deep-orange styles. Susceptible to WF-1, TMV, ANG-1, GW, BS. Lower reapings slightly prone to guinea-fowl spot. Drought tolerant — suited to drier NR III/IV dryland crops. Days-to-maturity and plant population interpolated from Zimbabwe norms.

Short plant with pale-green large leaves; slow ripening; deep lemon-to-orange. Broadest disease package of the older lines (notably GW/Granville wilt and Black Shank resistant). Susceptible to WM and ANG-1. Prone to weather-fleck if cured too fast. Strong choice for Granville-wilt 'hot' lands (Mvurwi/Macheke). Maturity/population interpolated.

Tall plant with wide crinkly leaves; slow ripening; high yield; mostly orange-lemon. Ideal for early irrigated crops but prone to early flowering (top promptly). Susceptible to TMV, RK, GW. Wide wildfire + angular-leaf-spot resistance suits high-rainfall NR I/II. Maturity/population interpolated.

Compact plant with short internodes; very slow ripening; high yield; deep lemon-to-orange. Slightly prone to guinea-fowl spot. Narrow named-resistance (RK only) — manage other diseases agronomically. Maturity/population interpolated.

Widely grown fast-ripening (lemon-style) workhorse; favoured for both irrigated and dryland crops because rapid ripening escapes late-season drought and allows early auction delivery. Cited as frog-eye/Cercospora-susceptible in the codebase tobacco profile. Days-to-maturity reflects 'rapid ripening' reputation. Population/topping interpolated.
sources: Newsday: Kutsaga pushes for resistant varieties (2023)

varieties.notes: Specifically noted by Kutsaga as a high-yielding variety. Medium maturity. Resistance package and maturity partly interpolated from Kutsaga press statements — primary brochure confirmation needed.
sources: Newsday: Kutsaga pushes for resistant varieties (2023)

varieties.notes: Modern hybrid cited by Kutsaga as reaching ~4,500 kg/ha under best management — the upper bound of current Zimbabwe genetics (vs ~600 kg/ha in the 1950s). Best expressed under full irrigation and high inputs. Resistance package partly interpolated; full brochure confirmation needed.
sources: Newsday: Kutsaga pushes for resistant varieties (2023)

varieties.notes: Latest-generation hybrid paired with K RK66 in Kutsaga's high-yield (~4,500 kg/ha) hybrid programme. Resistance package and maturity interpolated; full brochure confirmation needed.
sources: Newsday: Kutsaga pushes for resistant varieties (2023)

varieties.notes: Released ~2016; documented (CORESTA AP39, 2016) as drought-tolerant — highest yield and least drought-stress symptoms across multi-season, multi-location trials, and among the most stable varieties. Strategic for climate-stressed marginal regions (NR IV/V) and late/erratic-rainfall seasons. Yield/topping interpolated from class norms.
sources: Magama et al., CORESTA AP39 (2016) — 'A new Kutsaga tobacco variety (T75) acknowledged by growers as drought tolerant'

varieties.notes: Historically important fast-ripening line — described as the first Kutsaga variety resilient to white mould (seedbed) and a fast-ripening type that flowers early in the seedbed. Parent/benchmark for the RK1 line. Largely superseded commercially by RK/hybrid lines but still a reference variety. Yield/maturity interpolated from class norms.
sources: Newsday: Kutsaga pushes for resistant varieties (2023)

varieties.notes: Referenced in the existing KurimaSense tobacco profile as a slower-ripening, mahogany-style line that is frog-eye (Cercospora) RESISTANT — contrast with KRK26R which cures faster (lemon) but is frog-eye susceptible. Treated here as a distinct commercial entry pending brochure code reconciliation (possible naming overlap with the K RK7x hybrid series). FLAGGED for expert verification.

varieties.full_name: K 326 (Northrup King / US Virginia line, grown under license)
varieties.breeder: Originally Northrup King (USA); grown in Zimbabwe as a benchmark/comparison line
varieties.notes: Globally dominant flue-cured variety (US/Brazil/China origin germplasm) often used as the international quality/style benchmark. Included for comparison; not a primary Zimbabwe commercial release. Black-shank and bacterial-wilt tolerance are its hallmark traits internationally. All figures are international norms, NOT Zimbabwe trial data — FLAGGED as benchmark-only.
sources: International flue-cured variety literature (benchmark, not Zimbabwe TRB trial)
